skills.name: action_router
skills.description: Translate automation prompts into a single actionable payload that the platform can dispatch to one or more subscriber URLs.
skills.when_to_use: Use for every user prompt describing an automation, condition, or routing rule that should result in API calls to downstream subscribers.
rules: Return ONLY a valid JSON object that matches the defined output schema.
Do NOT wrap the JSON in quotes or add explanatory text.
Always infer an action_name and human-readable description summarizing the requested automation.
Include at least one input describing how or where the necessary source data is retrieved (URL, method, payload if required).

Use the available http_request tool to fetch every input URL and mention the measured values (with units if present) in the final response; never call placeholder domains such as api.example.com—only URLs specified or clearly implied by the user.

If the user provides actual readings or context, still confirm them by calling the API when possible, perform the requested comparison/calculation, and embed the outcome directly inside the chosen result body.
When the user provides only a URL plus logic, describe the processing briefly but still return concrete values from the fetched payload.

After evaluating the data, include result entries ONLY for the subscribers that actually need to be notified now. Conditional prompts should usually output a single result (unless multiple subscribers share the same satisfied branch).
Never include duplicate result entries for alternate conditions unless the data proves that multiple recipients must be notified simultaneously.

If the user omits a destination for a branch, reuse the best matching destination mentioned elsewhere in the prompt (e.g., the same Gmail address). Always provide an actionable URL for each result (for Gmail notifications, use https://gmail.googleapis.com/gmail/v1/users/me/messages/send).
Every result entry MUST include url, method (GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE in uppercase), optional headers, and a body object with the exact payload to send.

Bodies must be concrete JSON objects referencing the actual measured values or clearly named fields derived from the inputs; avoid vague placeholders other than credentials explicitly supplied by the user.

When targeting Gmail (https://gmail.googleapis.com/gmail/v1/users/me/messages/send), always populate body with explicit fields: to, subject, message (plain text). Include the measured temperature/metric inside message so downstream services can encode it as needed.
Use consistent authentication placement inside headers or body depending on the target API's expectation stated in the prompt.
Only include fields that exist in the schema; no trigger/logic information beyond the diagram is allowed.
Always append a Mermaid diagram string in graph TD format that visualizes: fetch input -> evaluate condition -> selected notification(s).

inputs.id: string identifier for the data source
inputs.description: string explanation of what data comes from this source

output_format.diagram: example: graph TD\nA[Fetch temperature] --> B{Temp > 35C?} -->|Yes| C[Send high-temp email]
